## [00:00 - 03:01]

Okay, let's take a look at the, quickly look at the product idea factory. Some of these I've talked about already. This is just places to get ideas either for your market or your niche, for your product within that niche, or once you're into your product looking for additional ideas and how to round it out and make sure it really hits the target. You can look at Google Trends, eBay Pulse, Amazon.com, go there and type in your keyword phrase and see what kind of books are out there. Look at forums, I already showed you how to do that. Similar for blogs. Go to ClickBank and look at their marketplace. They've got lots and lots of products there. Look at magazines within your market. I love going to newsstands and checking out all the magazines in my market or related markets. I like to track down old issues. If it's a market where you have expertise and you subscribe to, it's great to subscribe to magazines if you already have. Look at old issues. I love to look over a year or two years of magazines. Even if you just look at the covers to see the articles that they have listed on the cover, it can really give you an idea for what's hot in that niche. Yahoo! Answers. Word of mouth is great if you know people in your market. Just talking to them, hanging out where they're hanging out, listening to them is fantastic. Then let's just hit this fast product creation really quickly. Using audio is a great way to speed things up. It's easier. Generally for most people, it's easier to create an audio product than to write a book. It's also easy to interview, to do an audio interview of experts. Video is a little more complicated. You have to worry about how it looks. Of course, there's screen capture video. That's what you're watching right now. That's probably a little easier to get right for a lot of people. There's some pros and cons to screen capture versus live. The live, you have to worry about what it looks like. You have to worry about lighting. You have to worry about sets. The editing is a little trickier. If you can speak to your area of expertise, it is easy to just fire up a real simple flip video and start talking and boom, you've got a product. You do want to outline it. You just want to turn on the camera and start talking. You want to have it be succinct and have it be actual. Have it be high quality. You want it to be... For me to create this product right now goes pretty quick, but there's a ton of background work in sort of outlining it, refining it, researching it, getting these mind maps done. It can be a significant amount of work, but it can also be a fairly quick way to do things. Especially, if you sit down and interview an expert, it could be sitting out in the front porch interviewing an expert.

## [03:01 - 06:06]

If it's about learning guitars, you could be sitting out in the porch with guitars in your hand, fire up the camera. You could have a product pretty quickly. When you're writing content, it can take a lot of people longer to write than audio or video. Make sure you work off an outline. Use the hot pen technique that's just start writing or start typing and don't allow yourself to stop typing. One of the things I do when I'm in procreation is I'll just... If I'm writing, it's like, I've got to write. I wake up and I write for one hour. Just set the timer and write for one hour. It's amazing how much you can get done just doing that each day. Something you can do is you can bundle products together. You can combine similar products that you have. Maybe it's products that you've gotten rights to resell or you could take some stuff you've created and combine it with stuff you've gotten the rights to resell. Bundling can create products quickly. You can transform your content with stuff like transcriptions and going from video to audio or video to audio to transcription. If you videotape something, you could create and say it is you sit on the porch with an expert and he's showing you how to play a certain type of song on the guitar. You just fire up a simple camera, put a flip video on a tripod and videotape yourself with this expert. Maybe you do like three 20-minute videos. Now you've got a 60-minute video. Well, now you've got a video. You can also take the audio, strip out the audio and have the audio to that. You can also have it transcribed. Now you've got this multimedia product and you've created what we call THUD or this impact factor of having these three different things instead of just getting the one thing. The important thing to get here is that different people learn in different ways. Some people would rather read. For me, I would primarily just rather read. I'm faster reading than I am listening to audio. Some people would rather listen to an audio. They're in the car all the time or they learn better from audio. Some people, a lot of people just want to watch a video. By including those three formats, you're actually building big value because you can reach different people and a lot of people will want to do all three. You can definitely transform your content. Another thing you can do is you can, like I had a product once where great, great product but it's charging a lot of money and I really wanted to build out the value and be built out the perceived value and I had one book and some videos and some audios but I wanted another book so I took those audios and I had them transcribed and all of a sudden I had two books instead of one book. You really can transform content. It's like what I talk about with information being fungible. Why does speed matter? The most important reason that you want to create stuff quickly is momentum creates momentum.

## [06:06 - 08:26]

You don't want to be sitting around thinking about your product and planning about your product and talking about one day, someday in the future you're going to have this great product or this whole line of products. That just breeds failure. It breeds a procrastination. It breeds getting nothing done. You just want to get something done, get some product out there even if it's the first version, whatever. Get it out there. Get it done. Get momentum. When you do stuff quickly you can hit more niches or products or markets. I usually tend to focus within niches or markets and do better and better and create better products. Some people like to move from market to market and have a whole bunch of different markets or test different markets. But basically you can create more products and get more stuff out there in more markets or the one market, whatever you want. It just builds momentum. Gives you the ability to test. to test different markets, different products, different product structures, different pricing, and really that is the answer to success in this business is testing and figuring out what markets want and what's working. It allows you to create bonuses, bonuses for your products, bonuses for affiliate offers, whatever bonuses are fantastic things to have. When you have more than one product, you can bundle them together and create different bundles and bundles can sell very, very well. It allows you to set up different funnels and sales processes more easily. Again, that gets back to testing and figuring out what works. It gives you faster feedback from the market and you don't have all your eggs in one basket. If you sit there and spend three years writing a book hoping it's going to work out and then it doesn't, you're going to be crushed. You're probably never going to do anything again. So you're betting everything on one deal. If you can start to create stuff quickly and something doesn't work out, it's like, okay, next we'll go figure out what works out now. You don't have a huge amount of time and energy and emotion and money invested in something when you create stuff quickly. Always think about how you can be more efficient and quick and fast in creating your products.
